artifacts.content:
From: margaret.jennings@westbrookfumc.org
To: pat.morgan@email.com
Subject: Catching you up on everything (it’s a lot)

Pat,

Sitting here with my planner and a cold cup of coffee, trying to make sense of the next few months. Thought I’d write it all down for you instead of dumping it on you in little pieces over the phone.

First, church stuff: the leaks just keep reminding me they’re there. You remember when Evelyn slipped near the back doors during that heavy rain service on March 10, 2024? That same spot by the rear pews where she likes to sit when Parkview or Trinity View brings her over from Big Spring. Her PT at Scenic Mountain Medical Center was very blunt that another fall could mean a hip fracture and she might not walk again. We’ve still got non‑slip mats and that whole back section roped off until we can get the ceiling and cupping hardwood fixed.

And then there was Harold Pierce almost going down by the nursery hallway after the downpour on May 26, 2023, trying to step around the bucket with his cane. Ever since his minor stroke at Hendrick in Abilene he’s so unsteady. I keep thinking one bad fall and I’ll never forgive myself.

On top of that, Daniel came down on September 27, 2025 after those Labor Day storms and walked the sanctuary with Bill and me. He wouldn’t take his usual $650 inspection fee (bless him, he knows we’re still paying on Robert’s heart surgery at Hendrick), but his stamped letter says we’ve got 6–9 months to deal with the active leaks before we’re looking at mold trouble in the attic, especially risky for older folks like Evelyn on oxygen.

I’ll call later this week, but I wanted you to see the whole picture.

Love,
Margaret

metadata.subject: Catching you up on everything (it’s a lot)

artifacts.content:
From: margaret.jennings@westbrookfumc.org
To: emily.carter@email.com
Subject: Why I’m so worked up about these leaks

Hi Em,

I know you teased me Sunday about “living at the church,” but I want you to understand why I’m pushing so hard.

You remember Christmas Eve, December 24, 2023. That little puddle right inside the main double doors where the hardwood is starting to cup? When Caleb slipped and his asthma flared so fast Robert had to kneel down right there at the back of the sanctuary to calm him. For a minute I really thought we were going to end up at Hendrick Children’s in Abilene instead of finishing Silent Night. Every time I walk over that 6‑by‑10‑foot section I picture him gasping.

Then last summer during VBS (June 10–14, 2024), I had my 3rd–4th graders in Room 203 in the education wing when water started dripping right over the craft tables. I hustled them to the fellowship hall, but Angela Ruiz pulled me aside and said with Mateo’s immune issues being followed at Cook Children’s in Fort Worth, she wouldn’t bring him back if we didn’t get the roof and ceiling fixed soon. I don’t blame her.

And Hal walked Rooms 102 and 104 with Susan and me on November 3, 2025. The musty smell reminded him of that mold job he did at Trinity Baptist in Big Spring. He thinks we may be looking at $7,500–$10,000 in testing and remediation before VBS 2026, especially for kids like Caleb, since his pediatrician at Hendrick Clinic already warned us about damp, musty rooms.

So when I sound like a broken record about the roof, that’s why.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: Why I’m so worked up about these leaks

artifacts.content:
From: margaret.jennings@westbrookfumc.org
To: robert.jennings@email.com
Subject: Money (again) and the roof

Rob,

I’m at the church desk balancing things and my head is spinning, so I’m writing this before I come home.

First, I finally admitted to myself that between your December 2023 stroke at Hendrick in Abilene and all the rehab and meds, that $24,000 home‑equity line of credit on 214 Oak is going to be with us for a while. As part‑time bookkeeper here, I see the numbers every day and know I can’t just “give a little more” to the roof fund without risking default.

On top of that, I’m still sending the IRS $175 a month on that $6,300 payroll tax penalty from my April 2022 filing mistake. The repayment plan is set up through 2026. Add that to Pastor Miller cutting my hours last September to help squeeze the $128,500 budget, and there’s just not much left to stretch.

I also feel guilty about the church side. James and I are still working through the separate $4,600 payroll tax penalties from that 2022 withholding error. The confidential repayment plan is nibbling away at our cushion, which is why the finance committee doesn’t want to drain the last of the reserves for the $58,400 roof.

Meanwhile, we’re helping our own. Remember in September 2025 when Emily had to take three weeks unpaid from Cooper High for the postpartum depression? We covered $1,100 of her mortgage and daycare so she could see Lisa McMillan at West Texas Behavioral Health. And in July, after Michael’s BAH got reduced in that Dyess pay audit, we co‑signed that $7,800 loan at First Financial so he wouldn’t lose his car.

No wonder we’re tired.

Heading home soon.

Love,
M

Title: Roof / health / money – big picture

Jotting this down while it’s all in my head.

Health: Between March and May 2025 I was back and forth to Hendrick Medical Center in Abilene for this atrial fibrillation. Dr. Patel did the cardioversion and several follow‑ups. We’ve paid about $5,300 out of pocket from what used to be our emergency savings. Add that to Robert’s December 2023 stroke bills and his ongoing meds, and my own knees and fatigue, and it’s no wonder I feel worn thin.

Family obligations: Emily’s situation last fall still weighs on me. September 2025, she took three weeks unpaid from Cooper High because the postpartum depression was so bad. We sent $1,100 to help with mortgage and daycare so she could keep seeing Lisa at West Texas Behavioral Health Associates. Michael’s BAH got cut in that July 2025 audit at Dyess AFB, and we co‑signed the $7,800 loan at First Financial in Abilene so he wouldn’t default.

Evelyn: March 2024 at Parkview in Big Spring I helped her do the Medicaid recert; her Social Security is only $1,487/month now. Before she moved there in 2022 she tithed faithfully; now she simply can’t, and some of our key donors like Linda are already quietly sending $300/month straight to Parkview to help with her meds.

Church: All of this means our household and our “core” givers are stretched before we even talk about a $58,400 roof on top of a $128,500 budget and the lingering $4,600 IRS payroll penalty.

Remember: be gentle with myself when I can’t fix everything.

From: margaret.jennings@westbrookfumc.org
To: linda.harper@email.com
Subject: Quick follow-up after tonight’s meeting

Hi Linda,

Thank you again for staying late in the fellowship hall to go over the numbers. I know you’re as tired of looking at spreadsheets as I am.

I keep thinking about how much our “faithful few” are already doing. You, for one – that $300/month you’ve been quietly sending straight to Parkview Assisted Living in Big Spring to help with Evelyn’s upgraded medication plan since Michael’s BAH got cut at Dyess this January. Between that, your regular tithe, and the extra checks you slip into my hand at Christmas, you’re already carrying more than your share.

And Robert and I aren’t in a place to do more either. We redirected $2,400 last October that we normally would have tucked into the building fund so we could help Evelyn with the jump in her memory care fees at Sunrise in Big Spring after Dr. Hernandez at Midland Memorial diagnosed early‑stage Alzheimer’s. On my side, March–May 2025 wiped out our emergency cushion with that AFib workup and cardioversion at Hendrick under Dr. Patel.

All of that is before you even get to the church obligations: the $4,600 payroll tax penalties from my 2022 withholding mistake that you and I are still paying down with the IRS, and the $7,500 we’ve already set aside for the roof. It’s not that people don’t care about the building; they’re just stretched thin in every direction.

I’m praying that when we talk to the district, they’ll see that we are doing what we can, and that we’re not being careless, just human.

Gratefully,
Margaret

From: margaret.jennings@westbrookfumc.org
To: michael.jennings@email.com
Subject: Thank you (and a favor)

Hi Mike,

I printed out Capt. Hernandez’s email and put it in the trustee binder. Please tell her again how grateful I am that she took the time on October 8, 2025 to look at those ceiling photos from our old 1928 sanctuary, even if it was just an informal opinion from her 7th Civil Engineer Squadron account at Dyess. That sample scope‑of‑work she attached for older base chapels helped me explain to our folks why a full tear‑off and replacement makes more sense than another round of patching. When she mentioned that other base chapel where the chaplain nearly got in trouble for ignoring leak reports, I could see a couple of trustees sit up straighter.

I know you’re juggling your own mess with that pay audit and the BAH being cut in July. Dad and I don’t regret co‑signing the $7,800 loan at First Financial in Abilene for a second – we just wish the Air Force would get its act together so you’re not constantly playing catch‑up.

How are you feeling about Grandma Evelyn? When I helped her with Medicaid recert at Parkview in March 2024 and we wrote down that $1,487/month Social Security, it hit me how different things are from when she was still tithing regularly in 2022. Between that and Linda’s $300/month to Parkview, I’m realizing our “steady” givers are mostly propping up family these days.

Call Sunday if you can. I’ll be at the desk with my jar of peppermints, like always.

Love,
Mom

Title: Roof / insurance / trustees – working session
Date: 2025-10-15
Time: 6:30–8:30 p.m.
Location: First United Methodist Church of Westbrook – Fellowship Hall
Attendees: Margaret, James (treasurer), Linda (finance), Bill (trustees), Hal, Pastor

Notes:

– Review James Ortega’s visit on 10/10/25. He’s the senior adjuster from Lone Star Mutual who spent ~3 hours in the sanctuary, education wing, and attic taking photos and moisture readings. He told me privately in my office that if we don’t have at least two contractor bids in hand within 30 days, they may treat some of the ceiling damage as “ongoing neglect” and deny part of the claim.

– Confirm we’ve requested/received written estimates from High Plains Roofing and Covenant Roofing & Construction before 10/31. Attach copies.

– Go over Daniel Morgan’s structural letter from his 9/27/25 walk‑through with Bill and me. He waived his usual $650 inspection fee (knows we’re still paying off Robert’s heart surgery at Hendrick) but warned that if we don’t address active leaks within 6–9 months, we could be looking at mold in the attic and breathing trouble for older members like Evelyn on oxygen at assisted living in Big Spring.

– Discuss Hal’s informal $7,500–$10,000 estimate for possible mold testing/remediation in Rooms 102 and 104, based on his Trinity Baptist job.

– Financial reality check: remind team about $4,600 IRS payroll penalty repayment plan and limited reserves after $11,200 HVAC replacement.

Action items: assign follow‑up with insurance, schedule congregational info meeting, start draft of “Raise the Roof” campaign materials.
